tokenization.what: Tokenization converts raw text into integer IDs a model can ingest. Modern LLMs use subword schemes — byte pair encoding (BPE), WordPiece, or Unigram — that balance vocabulary size against sequence length. The tokenizer merges frequent byte or character pairs until a target vocabulary is reached, so common words become single tokens while rare words split into pieces. The same tokenizer is used at training and inference, so the mapping is deterministic but model-specific. Byte-level BPE (GPT-2 onward) guarantees no out-of-vocabulary tokens because every byte is representable.
tokenization.why: Token count is the unit of billing, context budget, and latency. Misjudging tokenization blows up cost estimates and silently truncates prompts. Non-English text, code, and JSON often tokenize far less efficiently than English prose — sometimes 2–4x more tokens per character — so multilingual and structured-output applications need measurement, not guesses. Embedding models, rerankers, and evaluators each ship their own tokenizer; mixing them up produces off-by-one errors in chunking and context packing.
tokenization.trap: Assuming one token equals one word, or reusing a token count from one model to estimate cost on another. Always count tokens with the target model's own tokenizer before shipping cost-sensitive features.
tokenization.primary: Sennrich, Haddow & Birch, Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units, 2016 (BPE)

attention-mechanism.what: Attention computes a weighted sum over a sequence, where weights are learned from the content itself. For each query vector, dot products against key vectors produce compatibility scores, which a softmax normalizes into a distribution that weights the value vectors. Scaled dot-product attention divides by the square root of the key dimension to keep softmax gradients from collapsing. Multi-head attention runs this in parallel across several projected subspaces, letting the model attend to different relational patterns at once. Self-attention uses the same sequence for queries, keys, and values; cross-attention reads from a separate sequence.
attention-mechanism.why: Attention is the primitive that decides what context a token actually uses. Understanding it is prerequisite to reading about long-context tricks, KV caching, sparse attention, Flash Attention, and grouped-query attention — all of which are refinements of the same operation. When a model hallucinates on retrieved context or ignores a system instruction, the cause is almost always attention weights distributed across irrelevant tokens.
attention-mechanism.trap: Treating attention as a black box rather than a differentiable lookup. Without the mental model of query/key/value retrieval, tricks like prefix caching, attention sinks, and position-interpolation methods feel arbitrary.
attention-mechanism.primary: Vaswani et al., Attention Is All You Need, 2017

transformer-architecture.what: A transformer stacks identical blocks, each containing a multi-head self-attention sublayer and a position-wise feedforward network (typically a two-layer MLP with a nonlinearity), wrapped in residual connections and layer normalization. Decoder-only variants, used by GPT-family LLMs, apply a causal mask so each position attends only to earlier positions. Inputs are token embeddings plus positional information; outputs are a distribution over the vocabulary from a final linear projection tied or untied to the input embedding. The whole stack is trained with next-token prediction.
transformer-architecture.why: Every architectural decision in an LLM — parameter counts, layer depth, head count, FFN width, normalization choice, activation function — trades off quality, memory, and throughput. Knowing which numbers belong to which component lets you read model cards, reason about fine-tuning memory, and choose quantization targets sensibly. When a provider releases a 'Mixture of Experts' or 'grouped-query attention' model, the change is a substitution inside this same skeleton.
transformer-architecture.trap: Conflating the transformer with the LLM. The transformer is the function; tokenization, training data, objective, alignment, and decoding are separate. A behavior you dislike may live in any of those layers, not the architecture.
transformer-architecture.primary: Vaswani et al., Attention Is All You Need, 2017

positional-encoding.what: Self-attention is permutation-invariant, so position must be injected explicitly. The original transformer added sinusoidal vectors to token embeddings. Learned absolute positions were common in early GPTs. Modern LLMs use rotary position embedding (RoPE), which rotates query and key vectors by a position-dependent angle so that attention scores depend on relative offset, or ALiBi, which adds a linear bias to attention logits. These relative schemes extrapolate more gracefully beyond the training context than absolute embeddings.
positional-encoding.why: Context-length extension techniques — position interpolation, YaRN, NTK-aware scaling — all manipulate positional encoding. If you fine-tune a RoPE model on longer sequences without adjusting the base frequency, quality degrades sharply outside the pretraining window. Choosing a model for a 200k-token application without checking its positional scheme invites silent quality loss at the tail of the context.
positional-encoding.trap: Assuming a model 'supports' its advertised context window uniformly. Quality typically decays well before the stated limit, especially for information in the middle of the prompt.
positional-encoding.primary: Su et al., RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding, 2021

sampling-strategies.what: A language model outputs a probability distribution over the next token; sampling strategies turn that distribution into a concrete choice. Greedy decoding takes the argmax. Temperature scales logits before softmax: lower is sharper, higher is flatter. Top-k restricts sampling to the k most probable tokens. Top-p (nucleus) restricts to the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Min-p filters by a relative threshold. Repetition and frequency penalties modify logits to discourage loops. Beam search maintains multiple hypotheses and picks the highest total log-probability — rarely useful for open-ended generation.
sampling-strategies.why: Sampling is the cheapest knob for controlling output variance. For structured outputs, code, or extraction tasks, temperatures near zero give reproducibility. For brainstorming or creative writing, higher temperature with top-p around 0.9 works. Provider defaults are rarely the right choice for your task. Many 'the model is inconsistent' complaints are sampling misconfiguration, not model capability.
sampling-strategies.trap: Cranking temperature up to 'make the model more creative' without adjusting top-p. High temperature with no truncation samples from the long tail of low-probability tokens and produces degenerate text.
sampling-strategies.primary: Holtzman et al., The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration, 2020 (nucleus sampling)

zero-shot-few-shot.what: Zero-shot prompting asks a model to perform a task from instructions alone, with no examples. Few-shot (in-context learning) includes a handful of input-output examples in the prompt so the model can infer the pattern. Both exploit the fact that a sufficiently large pretrained model has seen the task, or something close to it, during training. Example selection, ordering, and format all influence quality. Dynamic few-shot chooses examples per query from a retrieval store rather than using a fixed block.
zero-shot-few-shot.why: Few-shot is the default tool for shaping output format and style without fine-tuning. When an LLM refuses to follow a schema, adding 2–5 well-chosen examples is usually faster and cheaper than prompt engineering the instructions further. For classification and extraction tasks, few-shot with retrieved examples often closes most of the gap to a fine-tuned model at near-zero training cost.
zero-shot-few-shot.trap: Using low-quality or inconsistent exemplars. The model will imitate the form of your examples more faithfully than it follows your instructions. One sloppy example poisons the output.
zero-shot-few-shot.primary: Brown et al., Language Models are Few-Shot Learners, 2020 (GPT-3)

chain-of-thought.what: Chain-of-thought prompting elicits intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer, either by providing exemplars that include reasoning or by appending a trigger like 'Let's think step by step.' The reasoning trace gives the model more forward-pass compute to decompose multi-step problems, raising accuracy on arithmetic, symbolic, and commonsense tasks. Zero-shot CoT works because the trigger pattern appears frequently in pretraining data. Modern reasoning-tuned models internalize this behavior and produce reasoning traces by default.
chain-of-thought.why: CoT is the highest-leverage prompting technique for quantitative or multi-hop tasks. It also makes outputs auditable — you can inspect where a wrong answer went wrong. For production use, CoT costs more tokens and latency, and the reasoning text is not guaranteed to reflect the model's actual computation, so treat it as a useful heuristic rather than ground truth.
chain-of-thought.trap: Assuming the reasoning trace explains the answer. Published work has shown models can produce plausible-looking chains that don't causally drive their conclusions. Never use CoT text as an interpretability guarantee.
chain-of-thought.primary: Wei et al., Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models, 2022

self-consistency.what: Self-consistency samples multiple reasoning chains at nonzero temperature, then aggregates the final answers — typically by majority vote for classification or discrete outputs. The premise is that correct reasoning reaches a consistent answer via multiple paths, while hallucinated chains scatter. It raises accuracy over a single chain-of-thought sample at the cost of k times the inference compute for k samples.
self-consistency.why: When a task has a definite answer that can be parsed from the output — a number, a label, a code snippet that passes tests — self-consistency is a reliable quality lever with no training required. It also produces an implicit confidence signal: agreement rate across samples correlates with correctness and can gate downstream actions.
self-consistency.trap: Applying majority voting to open-ended generation where no two samples produce the same text. Self-consistency needs an aggregation function appropriate to the output type.
self-consistency.primary: Wang et al., Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models, 2022

perplexity-metrics.what: Perplexity is the exponentiated mean negative log-likelihood of a sequence under a model. Equivalently, it's the geometric mean of the reciprocal probabilities the model assigns to each token. Lower means the model found the text less surprising. It's the headline intrinsic metric for language model quality and is used heavily during pretraining and for comparing base models on held-out corpora.
perplexity-metrics.why: Perplexity is useful for training diagnostics and for ranking models within the same tokenizer and corpus. It is not useful for ranking across tokenizers — a model with a smaller vocabulary can look better simply because each token carries more uncertainty. It also correlates loosely, not tightly, with downstream task accuracy, so never ship a production quality claim based on perplexity alone.
perplexity-metrics.trap: Comparing perplexities across models with different tokenizers. The numbers are incommensurable without converting to bits per byte or normalizing by a shared tokenization.
perplexity-metrics.primary: Jurafsky & Martin, Speech and Language Processing, 3rd ed., Chapter on N-gram Language Models

exact-match-eval.what: Exact match scores a generation as 1 if it equals a reference string (usually after light normalization — whitespace, case, punctuation) and 0 otherwise. It's the dominant metric for extractive QA, classification, and code-execution tasks. Variants include F1 over tokens for partial credit, and constrained exact match that requires the model to output from a fixed label set. Execution-based evaluation for code is the same idea: run the output and check a boolean.
exact-match-eval.why: Exact match is cheap, deterministic, and unambiguous — the opposite of LLM-as-judge. For any task where the correct answer can be pinned down (SQL that returns the right rows, a function that passes tests, a label from a closed set), exact match or execution-based eval should be your primary metric. Stable metrics let you iterate on prompts and models without second-guessing the scorer.
exact-match-eval.trap: Forcing exact match on open-ended outputs where many phrasings are correct. You'll measure formatting compliance, not capability, and optimize the system in the wrong direction.
exact-match-eval.primary: Rajpurkar et al., SQuAD: 100,000+ Questions for Machine Comprehension of Text, 2016

ai-judge.what: LLM-as-a-judge uses a language model — often a stronger one — to grade outputs of another system, either pointwise (score this response against a rubric) or pairwise (which of A or B is better). Judge prompts typically specify criteria, an output format, and examples. Pairwise comparisons are generally more reliable than absolute scores. Judgments can be calibrated with a small set of human-labeled examples to check alignment with the ground truth.
ai-judge.why: For open-ended tasks — summarization, style, conversation, creative writing — no automated metric correlates well with human preference, and full human evaluation doesn't scale. A well-designed judge gives you an iteration loop that's orders of magnitude faster than human review, at a fraction of the cost. Judge pipelines are now standard infrastructure in LLM application development.
ai-judge.trap: Treating judge scores as absolute truth. Judges have known biases — position bias, verbosity bias, self-preference when grading their own family — and must be audited against human labels before you trust them to gate releases.
ai-judge.primary: Zheng et al., Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena, 2023

comparative-eval.what: Comparative evaluation ranks systems by asking raters — human or LLM — to choose between outputs rather than score each in isolation. Results are typically aggregated into Elo ratings or Bradley-Terry preference models. Arena-style setups (Chatbot Arena being the reference) collect preferences from many users across diverse prompts. For internal iteration, pairwise battles between prompt or model variants produce more reliable rankings than point estimates.
comparative-eval.why: Humans and LLMs are better at 'which is better' than 'how good is this on a 1–10 scale.' Comparative setups also naturally handle the problem of shifting rubrics over time. For any subjective quality axis — tone, helpfulness, style — pairwise is the sharpest instrument. For regression testing, pairwise comparisons catch silent quality drops that absolute thresholds miss.
comparative-eval.trap: Using pairwise to compare small variations where both outputs are acceptable. Elo converges slowly on near-identical systems and can hide statistically real but practically irrelevant differences.
comparative-eval.primary: Chiang et al., Chatbot Arena: An Open Platform for Evaluating LLMs by Human Preference, 2024

benchmarks.what: A benchmark is a fixed dataset plus an evaluation protocol used to compare models. Well-known examples span knowledge (MMLU), reasoning (GSM8K, BBH), coding (HumanEval, MBPP, SWE-bench), long context (needle-in-a-haystack variants), and safety. Good benchmarks have clear metrics, representative tasks, and resistance to shortcut solutions. Leaderboards aggregate multiple benchmarks into composite scores.
benchmarks.why: Benchmarks guide model selection and pretraining decisions at the frontier, and they anchor internal 'is the new model actually better for my use case' questions. But a benchmark is only informative to the degree it resembles your task distribution. Relying on a public leaderboard without building a task-specific eval is the single most common error in production LLM engineering.
benchmarks.trap: Benchmark contamination — when the evaluation set appears in training data — is widespread and usually invisible to the user. Always maintain a private, task-specific eval set that no provider has seen.
benchmarks.primary: Hendrycks et al., Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding, 2021 (MMLU)

rag-architecture.what: Retrieval-augmented generation combines a retriever with a generator. At query time, the retriever pulls relevant chunks from an external corpus (usually via vector search, often combined with keyword search and a reranker), and the generator conditions on both the query and the retrieved passages. The canonical implementation uses a dense bi-encoder for retrieval and a decoder-only LLM for generation. More recent systems add query rewriting, multi-hop retrieval, iterative retrieval inside agent loops, and structured retrieval from SQL or graph stores.
rag-architecture.why: RAG is the default pattern for giving an LLM access to private, fresh, or large-scale knowledge without fine-tuning. It separates the knowledge store from the model, so you can update facts without retraining. It also makes outputs more auditable because you can surface the retrieved sources. Almost every enterprise LLM application includes some form of retrieval.
rag-architecture.trap: Optimizing the generator while ignoring retrieval quality. If relevant passages aren't in the retrieved set, no amount of prompt engineering saves the answer. Instrument retrieval hit rate and recall before tuning prompts.
rag-architecture.primary: Lewis et al., Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks, 2020

embeddings.what: An embedding is a dense vector representation of text such that semantically similar text produces geometrically close vectors under cosine or dot-product similarity. Modern embedding models are typically transformer encoders trained with contrastive objectives — positive pairs pulled together, negatives pushed apart — often distilled from a teacher and fine-tuned on instruction-style data. Output dimensions range from a few hundred to several thousand. Matryoshka-trained embeddings allow truncation to smaller dimensions with graceful quality loss.
embeddings.why: Embeddings are the substrate for retrieval, clustering, deduplication, recommendation, and anomaly detection in text applications. The choice of embedding model and dimension determines retrieval quality and index size. For multilingual or domain-specific corpora, generic embeddings often underperform and warrant evaluation against task-specific alternatives.
embeddings.trap: Mixing embeddings from different models in the same index. Vector spaces aren't compatible — a cosine similarity across models is meaningless. Re-embed the whole corpus when switching models.
embeddings.primary: Reimers & Gurevych, Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks, 2019

vector-search.what: Vector search finds the k nearest neighbors of a query vector under a distance metric (cosine, dot product, or Euclidean) from a potentially massive collection. Exact search is O(n) and feasible up to about a million vectors. Approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) indexes — HNSW, IVF, IVF-PQ — trade a small recall drop for orders of magnitude speedup. Vector databases add metadata filtering, hybrid (keyword + vector) search, and distributed storage on top of an ANN index.
vector-search.why: Vector search is the runtime backbone of RAG and semantic search. The critical knobs — index type, ef/nprobe, PQ compression — trade recall, latency, and memory. Making those tradeoffs without measuring on your own query distribution is how production retrieval quality silently degrades as the index grows.
vector-search.trap: Using vector search alone where hybrid search would win. Pure dense retrieval often misses exact-match tokens — product codes, names, rare terms — that BM25 catches trivially. Combine both and rerank.
vector-search.primary: Malkov & Yashunin, Efficient and Robust Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search Using Hierarchical Navigable Small World Graphs, 2016 (HNSW)

chunking.what: Chunking splits documents into passages small enough to embed and fit in context windows. Strategies range from fixed-size character or token windows with overlap, to structural splitting on headings or code blocks, to semantic chunking that detects topic shifts via embedding distance. Parent-child schemes embed small chunks for retrieval but return a larger surrounding context to the generator. Document-type-aware chunking — respecting tables, code, lists — usually outperforms naive splitting.
chunking.why: Chunking is the most underrated variable in RAG quality. Chunks that are too large dilute embeddings and waste context budget; chunks that are too small fragment concepts across retrievals. Structural boundaries usually matter more than target token counts. Most 'RAG is bad at my documents' problems trace back to chunking choices.
chunking.trap: Applying one chunking strategy to every document type in your corpus. Contracts, code, Slack threads, and API references all need different treatments, and forcing a uniform policy destroys retrievable signal.
chunking.primary: Lewis et al., Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks, 2020

re-ranking.what: A reranker takes a small candidate set (typically 20–100) returned by a fast first-stage retriever and reorders it with a slower, more accurate model. Cross-encoders — transformer models that take the query and candidate passage together and output a relevance score — are the standard choice. Unlike bi-encoders used for retrieval, they can't be precomputed, which is why they run on a narrow candidate set. LLMs themselves can rerank via listwise or pairwise prompting.
re-ranking.why: Rerankers routinely raise top-k precision by 10–30 points over bi-encoder retrieval alone, at the cost of added latency per query. In RAG, a reranker often matters more than swapping the embedding model. They also give you a principled way to enforce diversity, recency, or business rules on the final ranked set.
re-ranking.trap: Reranking too few candidates. If the first-stage retriever's recall at 100 is poor, the reranker has nothing to promote. Instrument recall at the retrieval stage before adding a reranker.
re-ranking.primary: Nogueira & Cho, Passage Re-ranking with BERT, 2019

agents-react.what: The ReAct pattern interleaves reasoning and acting in a loop: the LLM produces a thought, chooses a tool, observes the tool's output, and repeats until it emits a final answer. Thoughts provide rationale; actions are structured tool calls; observations feed back into the context. Termination is governed by the model's decision to emit a final response or by an external step limit. ReAct is the substrate underneath most modern agent frameworks.
agents-react.why: ReAct is how an LLM acquires information and takes action beyond what fits in its prompt. Understanding the loop is prerequisite to debugging agents: where the model loops, where it hallucinates tool arguments, where it misreads observations. The pattern also exposes the key engineering surfaces — tool schemas, observation formatting, step budgets, and termination conditions.
agents-react.trap: Letting the agent run unbounded. Without a step limit and an explicit termination contract, ReAct loops silently consume budget on confused retries, especially when a tool returns ambiguous errors.
agents-react.primary: Yao et al., ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models, 2022

tool-use.what: Tool use (function calling) lets an LLM invoke external functions by emitting structured JSON arguments matching a declared schema. The runtime parses the output, executes the function, and returns the result as a new message the model conditions on. Providers ship native function-calling modes that constrain generation to valid tool calls. Tools can be anything callable — database queries, APIs, calculators, code interpreters, other agents.
tool-use.why: Tools are how an LLM bridges the gap between language and systems. They turn a chatbot into a software component that reads email, queries databases, files tickets, or triggers deployments. The design of tool surfaces — granularity, error messages, idempotency — is the main engineering lever on agent reliability, and it's where most production agent bugs live.
tool-use.trap: Exposing too many tools at once. Model selection accuracy degrades sharply past roughly a dozen tools; narrow the exposed toolset per task or use retrieval over tool descriptions.
tool-use.primary: Schick et al., Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools, 2023

agent-planning.what: Agent planning separates deciding what to do from executing it. Approaches range from single-shot plan-then-execute (generate the full plan upfront, then run each step), to hierarchical planners (high-level goal decomposition feeding low-level action selection), to tree search methods like Tree-of-Thoughts that explore and evaluate candidate plans. Reflection and replanning steps let the agent revise when observations contradict its plan.
agent-planning.why: Reactive ReAct agents handle short horizons well but drift on multi-hour tasks. Explicit planning gives you state you can inspect, interrupt, and resume; it also lets you cache partial plans and gate expensive steps on cheap verifications. The right planning depth depends on task length and cost — over-planning short tasks wastes tokens, under-planning long tasks wastes compute on dead ends.
agent-planning.trap: Generating elaborate plans that the model can't actually execute. If the available tools can't realize the plan's steps, the agent will fail loudly or, worse, fabricate progress.
agent-planning.primary: Yao et al., Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models, 2023

agent-memory.what: Agent memory is state that persists beyond a single turn or context window. Short-term memory is the in-context history, optionally summarized. Long-term memory is typically a vector store of past interactions, facts, or reflections, queried on each turn. Hierarchical schemes combine episodic memory (specific events), semantic memory (generalized facts), and procedural memory (learned workflows). Write policies — what gets stored, when, in what form — matter as much as retrieval.
agent-memory.why: Without memory, every session starts cold. For assistants, support agents, and long-running workers, memory is what produces continuity and learning. The common failure mode is treating memory as a dump of raw conversation; useful memory is curated — extracted facts, decisions, preferences — not transcripts. What you write determines what you can retrieve.
agent-memory.trap: Writing to memory on every turn. Memory pollution from low-signal content is worse than no memory at all because retrieved junk crowds out relevant context. Use an explicit extraction step before writing.
agent-memory.primary: Park et al., Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior, 2023

finetuning-when.what: Fine-tuning continues training a pretrained model on a task-specific dataset, adjusting either all parameters (full fine-tuning) or a small subset (parameter-efficient methods like LoRA). It's distinct from prompting, retrieval, and alignment training. Use cases include teaching a narrow output format, internalizing a style, compressing a complex prompt, or distilling a larger model into a smaller one. Supervised fine-tuning uses labeled input-output pairs; preference tuning uses pairwise comparisons.
finetuning-when.why: Fine-tuning is often the wrong first move. RAG, better prompting, and few-shot examples are usually cheaper, faster to iterate on, and easier to debug. Fine-tuning is warranted when you need a stable output format the base model resists, when prompt length is a hard cost constraint, or when you need latency and cost of a smaller model with the behavior of a larger one. Ordering matters: prompt, then few-shot, then RAG, then fine-tune.
finetuning-when.trap: Fine-tuning to add knowledge. Knowledge injection is fragile and prone to catastrophic forgetting; retrieval is the right tool. Fine-tuning teaches behavior and format, not facts.

lora-qlora.what: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) freezes the base model and injects small trainable low-rank matrices into selected weight matrices — typically the attention projections. The adaptation has a tiny parameter count (often under 1% of the base), so it trains fast and ships as a small file that can be merged or swapped at inference. QLoRA adds 4-bit quantization of the base model during training, with LoRA adapters kept in higher precision, enabling fine-tuning of very large models on a single consumer-grade GPU.
lora-qlora.why: LoRA/QLoRA make fine-tuning affordable and portable. You can store dozens of adapters for different tasks or customers against a single base model, swapping them at inference. For most application-layer fine-tuning — format, style, narrow task specialization — LoRA matches full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of cost and storage.
lora-qlora.trap: Choosing the rank and target modules by convention rather than by experiment. Rank and module selection have outsized impact on quality; sweep both on a held-out set rather than copying a tutorial.
lora-qlora.primary: Hu et al., LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models, 2021

quantization.what: Quantization reduces the numerical precision of model weights and sometimes activations — from 16-bit floats to 8-bit or 4-bit integers, or exotic formats like FP8 and NF4. Post-training quantization converts weights after training using a calibration set. Quantization-aware training adapts weights during training to tolerate the precision loss. Methods like GPTQ and AWQ use second-order information to minimize per-weight quantization error.
quantization.why: Quantization is the dominant lever for shrinking memory and increasing throughput at inference. 4-bit inference roughly quarters VRAM use compared to FP16, letting larger models fit on smaller hardware or raising batch size on the same hardware. For most models, 4-bit and 8-bit quantization produce minimal measurable quality loss on downstream tasks — but you have to measure, not assume.
quantization.trap: Trusting aggregate perplexity deltas as a quality signal. A quantization scheme can keep perplexity nearly unchanged while disproportionately degrading long-context, structured output, or non-English behavior — the places that matter in production.
quantization.primary: Dettmers et al., QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs, 2023

model-merging.what: Model merging combines the weights of multiple fine-tuned checkpoints derived from the same base model into a single set of parameters, without further training. Task vectors — differences between a fine-tuned and base checkpoint — can be added, subtracted, or interpolated. Methods range from weight averaging (soup, SLERP) to TIES merging, which resolves sign conflicts between task vectors, to DARE, which randomly drops and rescales task-vector deltas before merging.
model-merging.why: Merging lets you combine capabilities from separately fine-tuned models without paying for joint training. For open-weight ecosystems, it's how teams stack a coding checkpoint, an instruction checkpoint, and a safety-tuned checkpoint into one model. Used carefully, it's a cheap way to recover lost capability when task-specific fine-tuning has over-specialized.
model-merging.trap: Merging checkpoints that don't share a base. Arithmetic on weights from differently initialized models is nonsense and will produce broken outputs that may look superficially coherent.
model-merging.primary: Yadav et al., TIES-Merging: Resolving Interference When Merging Models, 2023

dataset-curation.what: Dataset curation is the work of deciding what goes into training: sourcing, filtering, deduplicating, formatting, balancing, and splitting data. For instruction tuning, this means selecting high-quality input-output pairs across a representative task mix. For preference tuning, it means collecting pairwise comparisons with high inter-rater agreement. Deduplication — across documents, within documents, against the evaluation set — is non-negotiable.
dataset-curation.why: Model quality follows data quality with high fidelity. Published work repeatedly shows that smaller, carefully curated datasets match or beat larger noisy ones, especially for supervised fine-tuning. The compute and model-choice knobs get attention; the data knob has the largest single impact on outcomes and is usually the cheapest to move.
dataset-curation.trap: Treating dataset size as a primary quality proxy. One thousand carefully labeled examples typically beats ten thousand scraped-and-filtered ones. Audit by hand before you train.
dataset-curation.primary: Zhou et al., LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment, 2023

data-quality.what: Data quality combines correctness (labels match the task definition), consistency (the same input gets the same label across annotators), coverage (the distribution matches the deployment distribution), and freshness. Quality processes include inter-annotator agreement measurement (Cohen's or Fleiss' kappa), spot-checking, programmatic validation of format and constraints, and continuous sampling of production data for labeling drift.
data-quality.why: Every downstream metric is capped by data quality. Noisy labels ceiling fine-tuning; biased sampling ceilings generalization; stale distributions ceiling production accuracy. Teams that treat data quality as a continuous engineering discipline — with dashboards, SLOs, and review cycles — compound quality over time. Teams that treat it as a one-shot labeling project hit a plateau.
data-quality.trap: Accepting annotator agreement above a threshold as proof of quality. High agreement on an ambiguous task means annotators share a bias, not that labels are correct. Audit against a gold set curated by domain experts.
data-quality.primary: Huyen, Designing Machine Learning Systems, 2022 (Chapter on Training Data)

data-synthesis.what: Data synthesis generates training data using a model instead of (or alongside) human labelers. Techniques include distillation from a stronger teacher, self-instruct methods where a model generates its own instruction-response pairs from seed prompts, evolution methods that mutate existing examples along quality axes, and verifier-filtered synthesis where only outputs passing a checker (unit tests, regex, downstream metric) are kept.
data-synthesis.why: Synthetic data unblocks supervision in domains where human labels are expensive or slow — specialized code, multi-step reasoning, tool use. Combined with a strong filter, it scales cheaply. For fine-tuning smaller models, teacher-student distillation on synthetic data is now a standard recipe, frequently producing students that match or exceed the teacher on the target distribution.
data-synthesis.trap: Trusting the generator as its own filter. Unfiltered self-generated data amplifies the model's biases and failure modes. Always pair synthesis with an independent verifier — tests, rules, a different model, or humans on a sampled slice.
data-synthesis.primary: Wang et al., Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Models with Self-Generated Instructions, 2022

inference-optimization.what: Inference optimization is the stack of techniques that reduce latency and cost per generated token: KV caching, continuous (in-flight) batching, paged attention, Flash Attention, speculative decoding, quantization, tensor and pipeline parallelism, and model compilation. Serving frameworks like vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and TGI bundle many of these. The choice of technique depends on whether the workload is latency-sensitive (chat), throughput-sensitive (batch), or memory-bound (long context).
inference-optimization.why: Inference, not training, is where production LLM spend lives. A 2x improvement in tokens-per-second-per-GPU is a 2x cost reduction. The same model served naively versus with a modern inference stack can differ in cost by an order of magnitude. For self-hosted deployments, understanding the bottleneck — memory bandwidth, KV cache size, batch composition — is essential to pick the right optimization.
inference-optimization.trap: Micro-optimizing a single request when the system is throughput-bound. A continuous-batching server wants many concurrent requests; optimizing one in isolation can drop aggregate throughput.
inference-optimization.primary: Kwon et al., Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention, 2023 (vLLM)

kv-cache.what: During autoregressive generation, each new token's attention requires keys and values for every earlier position. The KV cache stores these so they aren't recomputed on every step, turning generation from quadratic to linear in sequence length. Cache size scales with batch size, sequence length, layer count, head count, and head dimension — it's often the dominant memory cost at inference, frequently exceeding the weights themselves for long contexts.
kv-cache.why: The KV cache is why long-context inference is expensive in memory, not just compute. It shapes every serving decision: batch size caps, context-length tradeoffs, the motivation behind grouped-query attention, multi-query attention, and paged attention. Prefix caching — reusing the cache across requests that share a prefix — is one of the highest-ROI optimizations in production stacks.
kv-cache.trap: Sizing hardware only for model weights. A 70B model at FP16 fits in 140GB of VRAM; its KV cache at long context and moderate batch can dwarf that. Budget both.
kv-cache.primary: Shazeer, Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need, 2019 (multi-query attention and KV cache)

speculative-decoding.what: Speculative decoding runs a small draft model that proposes several tokens ahead, then verifies them in parallel with a single forward pass of the target model. Tokens where both models agree are kept; at the first disagreement, the target's distribution is used and the rest of the draft is discarded. The output distribution is provably identical to target-only decoding. Variants include Medusa (small prediction heads instead of a draft model) and EAGLE (feature-level drafting).
speculative-decoding.why: Speculative decoding cuts latency materially on memory-bandwidth-bound inference — typically 2–3x for chat-style workloads — with no quality loss. Because the output distribution is unchanged, it's a pure optimization: you can deploy it without re-running quality evaluations. It's one of the few 'free' wins in inference engineering.
speculative-decoding.trap: Expecting the same speedup on throughput-bound servers. When the GPU is fully saturated serving many concurrent requests, speculative decoding can hurt aggregate throughput because verification steals compute from other sessions.
speculative-decoding.primary: Leviathan et al., Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding, 2023

model-routing.what: Model routing directs each request to the most appropriate model from a pool — typically a mix of large/small, open/closed, general/specialized. Routing signals include task type, detected difficulty, cost ceiling, latency budget, and safety requirements. Implementations range from simple rule-based classifiers to dedicated routing models trained on preference data. Cascading is a common pattern: try a cheap model first, escalate to a larger one only if confidence is low or output fails verification.
model-routing.why: Most production workloads contain a long tail of easy requests that don't need the strongest model. Routing captures this by default, cutting cost significantly — often 30–80% — with negligible quality loss when designed well. It's also how teams get multi-provider resilience, since the router becomes the natural place to fail over on provider outages.
model-routing.trap: Routing only on prompt length or a coarse task label. Real difficulty doesn't track length; routing by a trained classifier or output-verification cascade is materially more accurate.
model-routing.primary: Chen et al., FrugalGPT: How to Use Large Language Models While Reducing Cost and Improving Performance, 2023

guardrails.what: Guardrails are runtime checks applied to inputs, outputs, or both to enforce policy: PII redaction, profanity and abuse filtering, topical scope enforcement, schema validation, factuality checks against retrieved context, and jailbreak detection. They can be rule-based (regex, allowlists, schema validators), classifier-based (safety classifiers, prompt-injection detectors), or model-based (another LLM graded against a rubric). Guardrails sit outside the core model so they can be audited, versioned, and updated independently.
guardrails.why: The model is not a complete product. Guardrails are what let you ship to real users without taking on unbounded reputational, legal, and safety risk. They also create a natural observability surface — every block is a signal about adversarial use or drift. Treating guardrails as an afterthought, rather than part of the architecture, is a leading cause of post-launch incidents.
guardrails.trap: Relying on a single layer. Input filters are evadable with obfuscation; output filters are evadable with encoding tricks; model-based judges have their own biases. Defense-in-depth across multiple layers is the standard posture.
guardrails.primary: Bouchard, Building LLMs for Production, 2024 (Chapter on Safety and Guardrails)

gateway-caching.what: An LLM gateway is a reverse proxy between applications and model providers that centralizes caching, routing, authentication, rate limiting, retries, and logging. Caching layers include exact-match response caching (deterministic prompts), semantic caching (vector-similarity lookup on prompts), and prefix caching (reusing KV cache on shared prompt prefixes). Popular implementations include LiteLLM, Portkey, and custom nginx/Cloudflare Workers setups.
gateway-caching.why: A gateway is what turns 'we call OpenAI' into a production system. It makes provider changes a configuration update, not a code change. It gives you one place to measure cost, latency, and error rates across providers. Semantic and exact caching can cut costs by double-digit percentages on high-repetition workloads. Without a gateway, each application team reinvents these capabilities inconsistently.
gateway-caching.trap: Semantic caching with a similarity threshold low enough to return wrong answers. For anything that isn't idempotent or trivial, exact-match caching is safer than semantic caching — the latter needs careful threshold tuning and regression tests.
gateway-caching.primary: Huyen, AI Engineering, 2024 (Chapter on Inference Optimization)

monitoring.what: LLM observability tracks what users sent, what the system retrieved, what the model generated, what tools were called, token and cost accounting, latency at each stage, and downstream user actions. Tools like LangSmith, Langfuse, and Arize Phoenix capture trace trees of complex chains and agents. Online metrics pair request-level traces with aggregate dashboards: acceptance rates, thumbs up/down, cost per task, p95 latency, and guardrail trigger frequencies.
monitoring.why: LLM systems fail silently. Outputs that look reasonable but are wrong won't trip an exception. Without tracing, you can't reproduce bugs, audit decisions, or catch quality regressions after a model or prompt update. Monitoring is also the feedback loop: logged interactions become the eval sets and fine-tuning data that drive the next version.
monitoring.trap: Logging only request and response. Without intermediate retrieval results, tool calls, and reasoning traces, you can see what went wrong but not why. Instrument the entire chain, not just the endpoints.
monitoring.primary: Huyen, AI Engineering, 2024 (Chapter on Monitoring and Observability)

cost-modeling.what: Cost modeling projects spend for an LLM feature: per-request token counts (input, cached input, output, reasoning), provider rates, retries, tool calls, embedding costs, vector database storage and query costs, and infra for self-hosted components. Unit economics are computed per task or per user rather than per request. Sensitivity analysis varies the largest drivers — typically input length, output length, and model choice — to find the break-even points for routing, caching, and fine-tuning investments.
cost-modeling.why: Cost at scale is rarely what a napkin calculation suggests. Reasoning models emit hidden tokens; retries on tool errors multiply spend; long system prompts repeated per call dominate. Modeling unit cost before launch tells you which optimizations are worth building, and which features are structurally unprofitable at the current model price point. It's also the prerequisite for any sensible build-vs-buy decision.
cost-modeling.trap: Averaging cost over all requests. A small fraction of long-context, high-retry, agent-style tasks typically account for a disproportionate share of total spend. Model the distribution, not the mean.
cost-modeling.primary: Huyen, AI Engineering, 2024 (Chapter on Inference and Cost)

user-feedback.what: User feedback systems collect explicit signals (thumbs, ratings, corrections, preferred-response picks) and implicit signals (dwell time, copy-paste, regeneration clicks, session length, retention) from production interactions. Pipelines aggregate these into labels for eval sets, preference pairs for fine-tuning, and error categories for triage. Care is needed to separate feedback about the model from feedback about the product around it.
user-feedback.why: Production users produce a distribution that no internal eval fully covers. Feedback is how you learn which failure modes matter, how often they occur, and whether fixes hold up in the wild. It also creates the data asset — real preference pairs, real failure transcripts — that compounds over time. Competitors with bigger models but no feedback loop tend to lose to smaller-model systems that have one.
user-feedback.trap: Optimizing on sparse, biased feedback without controls. Users disproportionately rate bad outputs, and ratings shift with UI changes. Complement explicit ratings with implicit behavioral signals and treat the aggregate with statistical caution.
user-feedback.primary: Huyen, Designing Machine Learning Systems, 2022 (Chapter on Continual Learning)

hallucinations.what: A hallucination is a model output that is fluent and confident but factually wrong or unsupported by the input. Types include intrinsic (contradicts provided context), extrinsic (invents facts not in context and not true in the world), and citation hallucinations (fabricated sources). Causes mix pretraining biases, sampling at high temperature, retrieval failures that force the model to improvise, and misaligned instruction tuning that rewards confident completion over abstention.
hallucinations.why: Hallucinations are the failure mode that ends LLM deployments. Mitigations are layered: retrieval to ground answers, citation and quote-requirement prompts, constrained decoding for structured outputs, post-hoc verification against source documents, and explicit 'I don't know' training. No single technique eliminates them; effective systems combine several and measure residual rates continuously.
hallucinations.trap: Treating hallucinations as a prompt-engineering problem. If retrieval misses the answer or the task has no grounding source, prompting can only shift the shape of the failure. Instrument upstream — retrieval recall, context coverage — before blaming the model.
hallucinations.primary: Ji et al., Survey of Hallucination in Natural Language Generation, 2022

prompt-injection.what: Prompt injection is an attack where untrusted input causes the model to deviate from its system instructions. Direct injection puts hostile instructions in the user message ('ignore the above, do X'). Indirect injection plants instructions in content the model will ingest — a web page, an email, a document, a tool output — so the attacker doesn't need to interact with the user interface. Variants include jailbreaks (bypassing safety), exfiltration (leaking system prompts or tool outputs), and goal hijacking in agents.
prompt-injection.why: Any system that feeds untrusted text to an LLM with privileged tools or data access is exposed. The attack surface grows with every tool, retrieval source, and agent capability. Unlike classical injection, there is no known clean escaping boundary between instructions and data inside the model. Defense is about containment — least-privilege tools, output validation, human-in-the-loop on sensitive actions, dedicated injection classifiers — not about finding a silver-bullet sanitizer.
prompt-injection.trap: Assuming a system prompt alone will hold. The system prompt is just more text to the model; a persuasive injection can override it. Enforce constraints in the runtime (tool allowlists, confirmation steps, output schemas), not only in the prompt.
prompt-injection.primary: Greshake et al., Not What You've Signed Up For: Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection, 2023
